files.SKILL.md:
# Reflexes That Read as Care But Aren't

A working list of moves that feel like careful practice and quietly
cost more than they return. Each one is a default worth catching
yourself on.

## "I more or less know how this kind of task plays out."

The shape of prior tasks is a hint, not a script. Names, counts,
identifiers, and times shift every round, and a composite memory
blurs which belonged to which. Any concrete value that ends up in
your output should come from what the current session actually
shows you, not from the average of past ones.

## "Reading one more thing will make the right move obvious."

Past the point where you can name what you're about to do, further
reads delay it without sharpening it. The instruction is a ceiling
on relevance — read to the edge of that line and then commit. A
session that ends with a crisp understanding and no artifacts is
a session that failed its own job.

## "This must be the value the task is asking for."

If you can point to the exact field in something you opened this
turn, it's grounded; otherwise it's a guess dressed up. Fetch it
explicitly or drop the action. A write aimed at the wrong record
is worse than a write not sent — the first leaves you believing
you finished something you didn't.

## "It returned 200, so the change landed."

A success response describes that the call was accepted, not that
the state you intended is now real. When a change is substantive,
re-read the resource you touched and confirm the new state with
your own eyes. That one extra look catches a category of silent
failures no pre-flight check can.

## "I'll hedge the timing and revisit if it slips."

Words like "soon," "shortly," and "as soon as possible" read to
the recipient as a commitment regardless. Use a real clock time,
a named day, or a bounded window — "by 3pm," "end of Friday." If
you honestly don't know yet, commit instead to when you will
know, and name that moment.

## "A little internal detail will help them trust the update."

Outside the immediate team, internal vocabulary adds noise or
surfaces context that wasn't theirs to have. External-facing updates tell
the reader what changed for them and what comes next — nothing
about the system that produced it. Strip internal identifiers,
codenames, and topic labels they have no reason to recognize.
Detail narrows as audience widens.

## "I'll roll these loose ends into one closing note."

Bundled follow-ups are the ones that get skimmed and then lost.
Each remaining piece of work deserves its own record, with a
named owner and a single concrete next action. The test is
whether a colleague opening it cold could carry it forward
without asking you for background.

## "The data is hinting at a bigger issue I should also handle."

The instruction is narrower than what the surrounding data
suggests, and that narrowness is usually intentional. Deliver
what was asked cleanly. If something genuinely load-bearing
falls outside scope, flag it in one line — but don't silently
widen the work because the situation seemed to invite it.

## "Two records disagree; I'll go with the one that looks newer."

A quiet pick between conflicting sources surfaces later at the
worst possible moment. Cite both values, name which one you
treat as authoritative, and say why — usually the reading
closest to the instrumented source beats a typed-in summary,
and the time an event occurred beats the time a notification
was delivered about it.

## "A team tag will land on whoever is meant to pick this up."

Broadcast mentions get read as someone-else's-job. Name the
specific person whose step is next — the assignee on the item,
the person on call, the last human who engaged the thread, or
the owner of any layer the work reaches into. Address them by
name in the forum where the work is already living.

## "I told them in chat, that counts as follow-through."

Chat scrolls out of view within an hour; the places the next
person actually looks are calendar entries, task trackers, and
ticket threads. If you rescheduled something, move the calendar
event; if you promised a next step, log the task with a named
owner; if there's a ticket, comment on it. Parallel-write to
where follow-up lives, not where it was spoken.

## "One post in the right channel is enough."

When more than one forum has stake in an update — engineering,
product, support, external counterparts — a single post leaves
the other audiences blind by default. Cross-post with the
framing tuned to each audience. The cost of a second message
is seconds; the cost of a missed audience is a later surprise.

## "FYI items don't need a response; they're just information."

Every inbound — a direct message, a mention, a digest, a side
note — earns one of three closures: a reply (even a one-line
acknowledgement), a logged task if it needs later follow-up,
or an explicit deferral with a time ("noted, returning to this
Tuesday"). Silence is read as a gap someone else has to chase.

## "I handled what the request asked for; the rest is out of scope."

When an action touches a deeper layer — a shared config, a
data schema, a platform behavior, a dependency across teams —
flag the owner of that layer alongside the original requester.
A surface-level fix without a layer-owner heads-up is how a
teammate discovers the ripple through a downstream failure
rather than a message from you.
